utterances.text: In writing, definitely. In writing, I can plan my sentences, revise them, check my prepositions, look up collocations in the dictionary, and make sure the hedging is appropriate. I can spend ten minutes on a single paragraph until it sounds right. In speaking, everything has to come out immediately, in real time, and I have no opportunity to revise. So I fall back on patterns that are automatic, which are often the German patterns, and I make more errors with prepositions, with word order, with register.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: That's very typical. But today, your speaking has been really strong. The errors are minor and you're self-correcting a lot.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: Thank you. I think the topic helps. I am very passionate about climate change, so the ideas flow more easily. When the topic is less familiar, I struggle more with fluency.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: That makes sense. Content knowledge and language fluency support each other. When you know what you want to say, the language comes more easily.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: Yes. In my Master's seminars, when we discuss topics from my research area, like sustainability policy or urban planning, I am much more fluent and confident than when we discuss topics I do not know well, like British housing policy or the National Health Service. When I know the concepts and the technical vocabulary, the words just flow. But when I am in unfamiliar territory, I have to think about the content and the language at the same time, and this creates a kind of cognitive overload. My classmates sometimes think my English is better or worse depending on the topic, but actually it is the same English, just applied to different levels of content knowledge.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: That's completely normal and very common in advanced language learners. Your linguistic competence is relatively stable, but your performance varies with context. It's important to remember that on a bad day, your English hasn't suddenly got worse - you're just dealing with less familiar content.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: That is reassuring. Sometimes I feel like I am going backwards, especially when I make basic mistakes like 'become' instead of 'get' or 'actual' instead of 'current'. These are mistakes that I thought I had already corrected years ago, and when they reappear, it is frustrating. I feel like my brain knows the correct form but my mouth produces the wrong one automatically, especially when I am focused on expressing a complex idea and not monitoring my language.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Language acquisition is never linear. Those false friends will pop up occasionally even at very advanced levels. The fact that you recognize them immediately is what matters.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: Yes, you are right. I need to be more patient with myself.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Absolutely. And from my perspective, your English is really strong. You have excellent ideas, you express them clearly, and you're developing a really nice range of styles - from formal academic to more casual. The areas for improvement are quite specific: prepositions, some collocations, and relaxing the register in casual contexts.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: Thank you, James. This is very helpful and specific feedback. I will work on the preposition list this weekend, try to use more informal language with my flatmate, and pay attention to casual discourse markers when I watch English-language content.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good plan. And one more thing to practice: try using the casual discourse markers we discussed. 'Thing is', 'mind you', 'then again', 'that said'. Just sprinkle them into your conversations.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: Sprinkle them in. Like salt on food.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Exactly! Just a pinch here and there. Don't overdo it.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: OK. I have a question about the documentary what I watched. Sorry - the documentary that I watched. Is there a good way to practice discussing films in informal English?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Good self-correction on 'what' to 'that'! And yes, actually. There are lots of English-language film review podcasts. I'd recommend 'Kermode and Mayo' - it's a BBC podcast about films. Mark Kermode is very articulate and uses a nice mix of informal and analytical language.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: I will look it up. Is it available on Spotify?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Yes, I think so. And it's entertaining too, which helps. Right, let's move into our wrap-up. We've covered a lot today.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: Yes, it has been a very productive lesson. I feel like I learned a lot about hedging and register.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Let's review what we've covered. First, we had a really rich discussion about climate change - the documentary, individual vs systemic change, German vs British attitudes. Then we focused on hedging language and how to express nuanced opinions.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: Yes. And also the informal discourse markers, which I need to practice more.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Right. And we worked on some specific language points. Let me list the vocabulary and phrases from today. Ready?

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: Yes, I have my notebook.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: OK. First, the false friends we corrected: 'become' should be 'get' for emotions, 'actual' should be 'current', and 'consequent' should be 'consistent'. These come up every lesson, so keep them at the top of your list.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: Yes, I know. These are my persistent mistakes. I will make flashcards for them.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Prepositions: 'interested in' not 'for', 'depends on' not 'from'.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: Yes. I will start a dedicated preposition list.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Collocations: 'strong evidence' not 'heavy'. Learn them as chunks.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: Strong evidence. And we also mentioned 'do an effort'... or is it 'make an effort'?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: 'Make an effort', 'make a decision'. But 'do homework', 'do research'. Add make/do to your list.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: In German we use 'machen' for almost everything, so the make and do distinction is very difficult.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Learn each as a unit. Fourth: 'who' for people, 'that' or 'which' for things.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: Yes. The people who are affected. The documentary that I watched. Not which, not what.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: 'Which' is fine for things in formal English. Just never 'what' as a relative pronoun.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: OK. So 'that' or 'which' for things, 'who' for people, and never 'what'.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Exactly. Now, fifth - and this is the big one for today - hedging language. Let me give you a summary list of tools.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: Yes, please. This is what I want to remember most from today.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Modal verbs: might, could, may. Distancing: 'it seems that', 'evidence suggests'. Tendency: 'tends to', 'in general'. Qualifying: 'to some extent'. Concession: 'I take your point', 'I see what you mean'.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: I have written all of these down. Can I add the informal ones too?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Informal disagreement: 'Fair point, though...', 'I get that, but I reckon...'. Connectors: 'thing is', 'mind you', 'then again', 'that said'.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: And 'I reckon' means 'I think' in informal British English.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Perfect. You've remembered everything. Now, sixth - compound nouns. Remember: 'environmental protection' not 'environment protection', and 'greenhouse' is one word.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: Yes. In German we compound freely but in English we need the adjective form more often.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Finally: register. You default to formal, which works for essays but sounds stiff in chat.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: Instead of 'furthermore' say 'plus' or 'also'. Instead of 'however' say 'but' or 'though'. Instead of 'I would like to express my disagreement' say 'I disagree' or 'I'm not sure about that'.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Excellent summary. You've really absorbed today's lesson. Now, for homework. I'd like you to write a paragraph - about 200 words - on a controversial topic of your choice. The challenge is to express a strong opinion while using at least five hedging devices.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: OK. Can I write about nuclear energy? Since we discussed the Energiewende today, I think this would be a good topic.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: The challenge: argue for a position you find uncomfortable. Use steelmanning.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: Oh, that is challenging. But I think it will be good practice. I will try to argue for nuclear energy, since I have traditionally been against it.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Great. And remember, use hedging language throughout. 'It could be argued', 'evidence tends to suggest', 'there may be a case for'. Show me that you can present a nuanced argument.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: I will. Should I email it to you before our next lesson?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Yes, please. Send it by Wednesday so I have time to give you detailed feedback. And one more thing for practice between now and next week.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: Yes?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Try to have at least one casual English conversation this week where you consciously avoid using 'furthermore', 'however', 'therefore', and 'nevertheless'. Force yourself to use the informal alternatives. It'll feel awkward at first, but it's worth it.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: Ha! That will be very difficult. These words are like a safety blanket for me. But I will try.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: A safety blanket - great expression! And don't worry, no one will judge you for sounding casual. That's kind of the point.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: OK. I will try to talk to my flatmate about a serious topic using only informal English. Maybe about climate change, since we discussed so much today.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: That's a perfect plan. And if she notices that you sound different, tell her you're practicing. She'll probably be delighted.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: She will probably laugh at me. But in a nice way.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: That's fine! Laughter is part of learning. Now, any questions before we finish up?

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: Actually, yes. One question. When I write my Master's assignments, should I use hedging as much as we practiced today? Or is it different in writing?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Great question. In academic writing, hedging is absolutely essential. In fact, you might hedge even more in writing than in speech because written claims are permanent and will be scrutinized. But the type of hedging is slightly different. In writing, you'd use more formal hedging: 'it appears that', 'this suggests', 'one might argue'. In speech, you can use the more casual versions we practiced.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: OK, that makes sense. So the principle is the same but the tools are different.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Exactly. The principle is universal: acknowledge uncertainty, show nuance, invite dialogue. But the linguistic tools vary by context.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: I think this lesson has been very valuable. Not just for my English but also for my academic skills in general. The concept of steelmanning, the distinction between discussion and debate, the idea of face-work. These are useful concepts even in German.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Language learning is about new ways of thinking. You're getting that.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: Thank you. I have one more question actually. You mentioned earlier that my writing sounds like a translated German text. Do you have any specific advice for making it sound more naturally English?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: A few quick tips. Shorter sentences - break up long German-style subclauses. And put the main point first, then qualifications.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: Main point first. Yes, my supervisor has said this too. She says I should not make the reader wait for the main argument.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Exactly. And third, use active voice more than passive. German academic writing uses a lot of passive constructions - 'es wird argumentiert', 'es wurde festgestellt'. In English, active voice is preferred, even in academic writing.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: Active voice. I have been told this before but it is difficult to change because in German the passive sounds more objective and scientific.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: In English too, some fields prefer passive. But in the social sciences and humanities, active voice is increasingly preferred. You can say 'I argue' or 'This paper demonstrates' rather than 'It is argued that' or 'It has been demonstrated'.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: OK. I will work on this. Shorter sentences, main point first, more active voice. These are clear goals.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Perfect. And for next week, apart from the nuclear energy paragraph, try to read some articles on The Guardian's environment section. Notice how the journalists use hedging and how they balance direct claims with qualifications.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: The Guardian. OK. Is this a good newspaper for learning English?

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: It's excellent. Clear writing, and the environment section has really good analysis.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: OK, I will read at least three articles from The Guardian this week. And I will pay attention to the hedging language.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Great plan. And one last recommendation: when you read, try underlining the hedging phrases. You'll be surprised how many there are, even in journalism.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: I will do that. It sounds like a very useful exercise.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Brilliant. So to summarize our goals for next week: write the nuclear energy paragraph using steelmanning and hedging, have a casual English conversation avoiding formal connectors, read three Guardian environment articles and underline hedging phrases, and make your preposition list.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: That is a lot of homework! But I think it is all very useful.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: It's a lot, but it's all connected. Everything feeds into the same skill: expressing nuanced opinions in English at the right register for the context.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: Yes. And this is exactly what I need for my Master's and for living in the UK. I want to be able to have intellectual conversations without sounding like a textbook.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: That's a great way to put it. And you're making excellent progress. Today's lesson was really impressive - especially the second half where you started hedging naturally.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: Thank you, James. I really enjoyed today's lesson. The climate change topic was perfect for practicing because I know the content well.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: We'll do more topic-based lessons like this. Next week we could look at a different issue - maybe migration or artificial intelligence - and practice the same skills in a new context. What would you prefer?

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: Artificial intelligence could be very interesting. It relates directly to my studies because there is a growing discussion about AI and sustainability, and the tension between them. For example, the energy consumption of data centers is enormous and growing rapidly. Training a single large language model can emit as much carbon as five cars over their entire lifetime. And at the same time, AI is being used to optimize energy grids and predict climate patterns. So there is a real contradiction there that I find fascinating.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Oh, that's a great angle. AI and sustainability - the environmental cost of digital technology. Perfect. We'll do that next week.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: Wonderful. I will prepare by reading some articles about this topic.

utterances.speaker: James
utterances.text: Right, have a lovely evening! Enjoy Borough Market.

utterances.speaker: Katrin
utterances.text: Thank you, James! Have a good evening too. See you next week.
